observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE):

Require the Inventory Committee to fast track and continue the conduct of a complete physical count of all supplies and materials to accurately ascertain their actual existence.

Require the Municipal Accountant (MA) and the Municipal Supplies and Properties Inventory Team to maintain complete records of ledger cards and property cards, respectively;

Review and reconcile their records to come up with reconciled balances of Inventory accounts; and

Adopt the use of Requisition and Issuance Slip (RIS) in each issuance of supplies and consolidate the RIS into Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued and furnish the Municipal Accountant the same to facilitate the timely and accurate presentation of the inventories on hand in the Financial Statements.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendations that:

The Municipal Mayor direct the Inventory Committee and the MGSO – Designate to include all types of properties in the physical count of PPE, submit the RPCPPE that includes all properties and coordinate closely with the Municipal Accountant for the reconciliation of the RPCPPE with the accounting records; and

The Municipality undertake the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances of government agencies as prescribed in COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020.

We recommended that the Management:

Assign a technically knowledgeable staff from the Municipal Engineering Office or from other departments to assist the MPDC in the preparation of the POW and other technical specifications of the PPAs under the 20% DF;

Maximize the utilization of the 20% Development Fund for socio-economic developments; and


Thoroughly evaluate the proposed projects ensuring that all factors are considered prior to approval in order to minimize delays and reprogramming of PPAs.

We recommended that the Management:

Implement the projects funded by the fund transfers in order to provide the intended benefits to the public;


Review the Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) for the said fund transfers in order to determine if there are special provisions regarding unexpended balances; and


Return the unexpended balances of the fund transfers to the respective source agencies if there are no provisions provided in the MOA that such balances should be retained by the Municipality.

We recommended that:

The Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Engineer to timely submit to the Audit Team the complete information on all ongoing government projects, programs and activities (PPA) as well as those that are to be implemented during the year as required under COA Circular No. 2013-004; and

The Municipal Engineer coordinate with the Municipal Accountant for the preparation and submission of the monthly project status reports of various government PPAs implemented by the Municipality.

We recommended that the Management revise its Municipal Revenue Code in order to strengthen the Municipality’s revenue generating capacity so that more funds will be available for the implementation of the government’s programs, projects and activities in accordance with RA No. 7160.

We recommended that the Management:

Conduct proper planning by considering all planned activities for the year and anticipate occurrence of irregular activities to determine the adequate number of bonded personnel and the reasonable amount of fidelity bond to be applied for each accountable officer; and

Consider adopting an Automated Teller Machine (ATM) payroll system for the salaries and wages of municipal employees and job orders to reduce the number and amount of cash advances of the Administrative Officer V/Cashier as well as provide greater convenience, transparency and accountability.

We reiterated our previous recommendation that the Municipal Accountant require the submission of the FURs and the full liquidation of the fund transfers granted to various NGOs/POs in compliance with COA Circular Nos. 2007-001 and 2016-005.

We reiterated our previous recommendation that the Municipal Accountant require the submission of the FURs and the full liquidation of the fund transfers granted to the concerned barangays in compliance with COA Circular Nos. 94-013 and 2016-005.

We recommended that the management immediately enforce the immediate settlement of all audit disallowances exceeding three years as of December 31, 2013 pursuant to Section 7.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009.
We also recommended that monthly deduction in the payroll of persons liable be effected to conform with COA policy regarding the settlement of disallowances through installment payment.  The payment period shall be for 36 months only. 

We further recommended that the accounting personnel exert effort to look into the difference between Receivable-Disallowances/charges account reflected in the financial statement and reports issued by Auditor such as RADC.  Henceforth, always reconcile the balance of the said account with RADC and SASDC issued by the auditor quarterly.

adjusting journal entries to correct the misstatements in Receivables and
Revenue accounts:

b) advise the OIC-MT to regularly review and monitor the Summary of RPT
Delinquencies and ensure the correctness of the data by validating the
computations as provided under the existing rules and regulations;
c) enjoin the MA and the OIC-MT to:
i. reconcile the discrepancy between the amounts credited to the RPT
and SET Receivables and the collections recorded in the SRS of the
MTO; and
ii. exclude penalties in the RPT and SET Receivables account and ensure
compliance with Sections 20 and 23 Chapter 3 Volume I of MNGAS
for LGUs, the definition of taxes in the IPSAS 23, and COA Circular
No. 2015-009 in setting up RPT and SET Receivables to facilitate the
fair presentation of these accounts in the FS;
d) direct the ME, as the Building Official, to inform all applicants of building
permit of their post-construction responsibilities, including the mandatory
filing of a sworn declaration of the true value of the structure upon
completion; and
e) require the OIC-Municipal Assessor to declare all identified undeclared
properties in the name of the defaulting owner, if known, or under an
“unknown owner” classification, as the case may be, and assess them for
taxation in accordance with existing laws, rules, and regulations.

We recommended the LCE to:
a) instruct the Inventory Committee to:
i. perform a periodic physical inventory count of supplies and
materials; and
ii. prepare the corresponding RPCI for submission to the AT not later
than July 31 and January 31 of each year for the first and second
semesters, respectively;
b) require the GSO to:
i. maintain a complete and updated record of stock cards for each
inventory item for reconciliation with the ledger cards of the MAO;
ii. closely coordinate with the MAO for the conduct of thorough
verification and reconciliation of the RPCI and accounting records to
identify and resolve the nature and causes of any variance noted,
holding the designated custodians accountable and/or responsible for
the discrepancies; and
iii. jointly coordinate with the Municipal Health Officer for the regular
submission of copies of AIR, DR, RIS, SSMI, sales invoices/billings,
and other similar documents to the MAO for the timely recording of
drugs and medicines in the books of the Municipality;
c) direct the MAO to ensure that all JEVs drawn to recognize receipts and
issuances of inventories are supported with valid and sufficient
documentation; and
d) enjoin the foregoing concerned Offices to adopt written internal policies
and guidelines relative to accounting, reporting, and monitoring of drugs
and medicines.

We recommended the LCE to:
a) instruct the Inventory Committee to:
i. devise the PIP for the conduct of physical inventory of all PPE at the
most reasonable time, including the preparation of RPCPPE with its
pertinent property records, and submit a copy of the same to the AT
not later than January 31 of each year. The RPCPPE shall be
reconciled with the PPE records or ledger maintained by the MAO,
and any discrepancy should be adjusted accordingly; and
ii. conduct the one-time cleansing of PPE in close coordination with the
GSO and the MAO to establish the veracity and propriety of the
balances of PPE accounts; and
b) require the GSO to:
i. prepare the corresponding Inventory and Inspection Report of
Unserviceable Property for the identified unserviceable properties
that remained and were recorded in the books, and initiate the
process of disposing of the same;
ii. maintain efficient property management by following the prescribed
procedures in property recording and maintaining accurate property
cards;
iii. ensure that PARs are prepared, issued to end-users, and regularly
updated/renewed every three years;
iv. assign property tags/reference numbers for each PPE item during the
physical inventory count to easily identify and trace the location of
the subject PPE;
v. undergo training on supply and property management being
conducted by other government agencies like DBM and COA to keep
abreast and broaden her understanding of her responsibilities and
accountabilities on property management;
vi. apply and/or renew the registration of all serviceable motor vehicles
and construction and heavy equipment with the LTO and contracts
of insurance from the GSIS to avoid additional expenses in terms of
penalties and payment of damages to third-party liabilities in case of
vehicular accident or other casualty; and
vii. monitor regularly if those subject assets are renewed yearly with the
LTO and have appropriate insurance coverage from the GSIS.

We recommended the LCE to instruct the MAO to:
a) maintain the appropriate CIPLC for each specific type of asset
constructed, whether through straight contract or by administration;
b) ensure that all the required data in the CIPLC are properly indicated
therein and henceforth, reclassify the total CIP cost to the proper asset
account using the data in the CIPLC as a reference upon completion of the
project; and
c) prepare the following necessary adjusting journal entries to correct the
misstatements in Asset, Liability, and GE accounts:

infrastructure project

We recommended the LCE to:
a) instruct the MA to:
i. draw the following accounting entries to correct the misstatements in
Leave Benefits Payable and GE accounts:
Particulars Dr. Cr.
Government Equity ₱1,463,090.85
Leave Benefits Payable ₱1,463,090.85
ii. reclassify the non-current portion of the Leave Benefits Payable for
the proper presentation of the account in the SFP; and
iii. ensure that all claims for monetization of leave credits are duly
supported with the necessary documentary requirements provided
under COA Circular No. 2012-001;
b) direct the HRMO to:
i. strictly comply with the provisions of CSC MC No. 41, s. 1998 and No.
16, s. 2002 on the monetization of the leave credits of employees.
Likewise, discontinue the practice of allowing the monetization of sick
leave credits unless warranted by the circumstances provided under
CSC MC No. 16, s. 2002;
ii. initiate and prioritize the reconciliation and correction of balances in
the leave cards; and
iii. revisit its process for updating and maintaining leave cards.
Specifically, the revised process should include regularly updating
leave cards and other internal control measures to provide timely and
accurate updates;
c) enjoin the MA and the HRMO to institute a periodic audit of all employees’
accumulated leaves before approval and payment of their respective
monetized leave credits; and
d) set aside additional reserve funds for the accumulated compensated leave
credits as of year-end; henceforth, ensure the adequacy of the funds
provided for the leave credits earned thereafter by including the said
benefits in the annual budget.

We recommended the LCE to:
a) instruct the MA to:
i. draw the following accounting entries to correct the overstatements in
Trust Liabilities – DRRMF and GE accounts:
Particulars Dr. Cr.
Trust Liabilities – DRRMF ₱6,713,778.86
Prior Period Adjustments ₱6,713,778.86
ii. perform a thorough review of all disbursement claims to ensure that
these are properly supported with proper and complete
documentation before processing payment; and
b) require the Municipal DRRM Officer to ensure full compliance with the
prescribed minimum standards for disaster preparedness set by the DILG
and LGA, as adopted by the NDRRMC.

We recommended the LCE to:
a) instruct the MSWDO to establish an updated centralized database for
tracking repeat or recurring clients to facilitate the efficient identification,
validation, and verification processes needed for financial aid programs;
b) furnish the AT with the certified true copy of the unsubmitted MOA/TA
with DOLE for validation, and henceforth, ensure that all funds received
from SAs are supported with a duly signed MOA/TA to clearly define the
specific guidelines and procedures in the implementation of individual
projects, including the responsibilities of the Municipality, thereby
ensuring timely accomplishment thereof for the benefit of the intended
beneficiaries.

We recommended the LCE to instruct the MAO to reconcile its records to
determine the nature and necessary details of the ₱312,338.02 balance in the Due to
BIR account and, henceforth, remit to the BIR the withholding taxes or prepare the
necessary JEVs to adjust the books of accounts, if warranted.

We recommended the LCE to instruct the MA to:
a) consistently comply with PhilHealth rules and regulations on the proper
deduction of PhilHealth premium contributions of its personnel and the
remittance thereof within the prescribed period; and
b) verify and reconcile the unaccounted balance of the Due to PhilHealth
account amounting to ₱12,839.75 and immediately effect the necessary
adjustments accordingly.

We recommended the LCE to instruct the MA to:
a) consistently comply with GSIS rules and regulations on the proper
deduction of GSIS premium contributions and loan amortizations of its
personnel and the remittance thereof within the prescribed period; and
b) verify and reconcile the unaccounted balance of the Due to GSIS account
amounting to ₱226,553.51 and immediately effect the necessary
adjustments accordingly.

We recommended the LSB to:
a) ensure that the date of transmittal of the copy of the SEF Budget to the
concerned departments is indicated in the receiving copy on file;
b) submit the quarterly and annual reports using the SEF Budget
Accountability Form No. 1 (Annex B) on SEF utilization, as prepared by
the MA, to the DepEd CO, through appropriate channels, with a copy
furnished to the local sanggunian concerned and Regional Offices of the
DBM and DILG; and
c) ensure that the Quarterly Utilization Report was regularly posted in three
conspicuous public places in the Municipality.

We recommended the LCE to:
a) issue an appropriate policy directive, copy furnished to the DILG Office
that conducted the review of the Municipality’s GPB, to ensure the
dissemination and implementation of the GPB; and shall mandate the
Municipal GFPS to: i) provide technical assistance in the implementation
of the GPB; ii) monitor its implementation; iii) ensure the preparation and
submission of GAD AR; and iv) consolidate reports on the implementation
of the GPB; and
b) instruct the GFP to submit the documentary requirements supporting the
GAD AR.

We recommended that the:
Municipal Treasurer obtain the necessary information/documents to support the identified reconciling items and submit the same to the Municipal Accountant to facilitate the adjustments of the reconciling items in the books of accounts; and
Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary adjusting entries to take up the reconciling items in the books of accounts to ensure fair presentation of the financial statements.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE):
Instruct the AOs for the liquidation of cash advances as required under COA Circular No. 97-002; and
Direct the Municipal Accountant to continuously require/demand the concerned AOs of the immediate settlement of the remaining unliquidated cash advances and if necessary, withhold the salaries of the concerned officials and employees for the settlement thereof.

We recommended that the LCE:
Direct the Accounting Office and MGSO/Supply Officer to conduct henceforth periodic reconciliation of inventory records to promptly address any discrepancy; and
Direct the Inventory Committee to reconcile the existing variance between the stock cards and/or RPCI against the accounting records, and prepare adjusting entries if warranted, supported with proper documentation.

We recommended that the Inventory Committee, in coordination with the Municipal Accountant, follow the procedural guidelines provided under COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 for the reconciliation of inventory count per RPCPPE with property and accounting records to establish the correct balance of PPE.

We recommended that the LCE:
Direct the concerned offices to prepare formal inquiries to the suppliers, notifying them of their outstanding claims from the LGU;
If upon validation, no actual claim has been filed by the creditors, require the concerned office to issue a certification addressed to the Municipal Accountant to facilitate the adjustment thereof in the books of accounts; and
Direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary adjusting entries to take up the settled accounts payable in the books of accounts to ensure fair presentation of the financial statements.

We recommended that the LCE direct the Municipal Accountant to reclassify the portion of the long-term loan which is due for settlement within 12 months from reporting date as Current Liabilities in the Statement of Financial Position.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
In coordination with other offices concerned, prepare timetable to prioritize the evaluation, of dormant Other Payables accounts for review, analysis and adjustments; and
Make the necessary adjusting entries for misclassified transactions to ensure accuracy of affected account balances in the financial statements.

We recommended that the LCE require the HRMO to:
Indicate in the JO Contract a clear and specific delineation of duties and responsibilities, as well as expected output/deliverables of the JO workers; and
Assess and evaluate its existing organizational structure and prepare a staffing pattern plan to appropriately determine the ideal number of plantilla items/positions needed to efficiently and effectively carry out the Municipality’s operations to avoid excessive hiring of JO workers and mitigate the incurrence of additional cost.

We recommended that the LCE:
Direct the GAD Focal Point System to prepare a GAD Plan and Budget containing attainable PPAs with more accurate fund allocation to ensure that all PPAs will be carried out in accordance with the approved plan; and
Require the Department Heads of lead or responsible offices to facilitate full implementation of PPAs assigned to them to fully utilize the allocated funds.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant reconcile the amount presented in the DSFPos and Notes to Financial Statements of the TL-DRRMF account and effect the necessary adjusting/correcting entries.

We recommended that the Management through the MDRRM Officer:
Ensure the efficient and effective utilization of the LDRRMF through implementation of programs and projects pertaining to disaster prevention, mitigation, preparedness, response, rehabilitation and recovery devised to avoid, mitigate or limit, and recover from the potential adverse impact of disasters; and
Adopt a well-defined strategy by enhancing the present monitoring and review procedures during the planning, implementation and program evaluation, to ensure proper and timely execution of the programs and projects thereby realizing the benefits provided in RA No. 10121.

We recommended that the MPDC and Municipal Engineer maximize the utilization of the 20 per cent LDF by ensuring that all funded projects are well-planned and procurement-and-implementation-ready through continuous improvements in planning, programming, and project execution to achieve desirable socio-economic development outcomes for the community.

We recommended that the LCE direct the BAC to strictly enforce the requirement for contractors to post the prescribed performance security as mandated by Section 39.1 of R-IRR of RA No. 9184 and submit copies to COA of Performance Securities for both ongoing and recently awarded infrastructure projects, as well as for completed projects that have not yet reached one year from their completion or final acceptance for audit verification.

We recommended that the Management:
Require the contractors to post warranty securities covering the required period and amount provided under Section 62.2.3 of the R-IRR of RA No. 9184, otherwise, structural defects and or failure within the warranty period not covered by the surety bond and not repaired by the contractor shall be borne by persons liable;
To ensure contractor’s compliance to the requirements of the law, require the concerned office to implement a sound and effective monitoring system to guarantee reimbursement of expenses in restoration or reconstruction work in case of Structural Defects/Failure occurring during the applicable warranty period; and
The Municipal Accountant should ensure that disbursements for final payment of completed infrastructure projects are supported with the necessary warranty security.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
1. Prepare the necessary adjusting entries to reflect the correct unremitted balances of the affected accounts at year-end, ensuring their fair presentation in the financial statements;
2. Discontinue the practice of transferring account balances at the end of the year; and
3. Conduct periodic reconciliation of account balances, closely monitor the preparation of the needed schedules for the remittance of the withheld/deducted amounts and ensure its remittance in full within the prescribed deadlines.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) direct the Municipal Accountant to:
1. Ensure consistent presentation of combo bank accounts in both the General Ledger and the Bank Reconciliation Statements by transferring the SEF balance of ₱803,236.74 to the TF/SEF combo account; and
2. Disclose in the Notes to Financial Statements any shared bank accounts used for multiple funds (e.g., TF/SEF combo accounts), specifying the respective fund allocations.
We also recommended strict compliance with Section 111(2) of PD 1445 and Paragraph 27 of IPSAS 1.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE):
1. Strictly enforce the laws, rules and regulation on the grant, utilization and liquidation of cash advances;
2. Require the Municipal Accountant to send notices 10 days before expiration of the 30-day period to concerned personnel to settle their accounts or face administrative and legal sanctions for non-compliance; and
3. Consider a request for write-off of the dormant or unliquidated cash advances aging 10 years and more totalling ₱7,038.03 following the specific guidelines and procedures under Items 7.0 and 8.0 of COA Circular No. 2023-008 dated August 17, 2023. However, it is emphasized that: (a) as appropriately defined, writing off of dormant accounts is only a process of derecognizing the asset and the corresponding allowance for impairment from the books of accounts; (b) under Item 11.0 of the COA Circular, it shall not be interpreted to neither condone the written off accounts nor to extinguish the obligations; and (c) Management shall continue to exert effort to collect the accounts appearing in the RAWO or RDDA when circumstances warrant.

We recommended that the LCE direct the Municipal Accountant to:
1. Closely coordinate with the source agency, secure documents to reconcile the LGU’s books with DOH and NFA to arrive at a correct balance of the Due to NGAs- DOH and Due to Other GOCCs-NFA account; and
2. Facilitate the re-submission of the liquidation reports, if any to DOH RO. If none, the Municipal Accountant prepare and submit the liquidation reports to DOH RO to ensure proper recording and reconciliation of accounts. Additionally, the Municipal Accountant consistently coordinate with the source agency to verify that all the liquidation reports submitted by the LGU have been properly recorded in the agency’s records.
We further recommended that the Municipal Government ensure that all future transactions are properly supported by complete documentation and formal agreements duly signed by the authorized officials to facilitate accurate recording and accountability.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that obligations made are properly accounted for. We further recommended to resubmit the LDRRMF Utilization report showing the correct ending balances in strict compliance with Section 111 (2) of PD 1445.

We recommended that the LCE direct the MAO to:
1. Reclassify the amount of ₱1,305,717.70 from Other Payables to Trust Liabilities – DRRMF to reflect the proper accounting treatment of the donations received for disaster-related purposes; and
2. Ensure that all donations intended for disaster risk reduction and management are properly recorded in accordance with the accounting guidelines prescribed under Annex C of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.

We recommended that the LCE take immediate steps to ensure that the unutilized SWM equipment is put to use in order to enhance the Municipality's waste management capabilities, otherwise, it will be a missed opportunity to improve environmental practices.
We also recommended that the LCE require the MENRO Designate to conduct a review of the Deed of Donation and related MOA entered into with the DENR-EMB V, identify the areas of concern and recommend action/s to ensure the Municipality’s compliance with the salient provisions thereof.
We further recommended that the LCE strictly comply with Section 2 of P.D. No. 1445 to guarantee that all government resources are efficiently, economically and effectively managed, expended and utilized in accordance with the relevant laws, rules and regulations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the concerned municipal officials:
1. Discontinue the payment of registration fees to various leagues and associations for the conduct of regular and routinary meetings;
2. Consider proposing to their respective leagues that the conduct of 1-day meetings may be done through teleconferencing to eliminate the incurrence of traveling expenses and registration fees, or holding them at government-operated function halls, session halls and event centers to minimize costs; and
3. Exercise due prudence in the disbursement of government funds to avoid incurrence of unnecessary expenditures.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) instruct the MT and the RAAFs to prepare the monthly RAAFs to be attached to the CRAAFs for submission to the AT not later than the fifth day of the ensuing month in accordance with Sections 50 and 51 of NGAS Manual for LGUs.
We also recommended to stop the practice of sharing booklets of AFs. The transfer of AFs from one CO to another shall be supported with proper recording so that accountability to the AFs could be later established.

We recommended that the LCE require the MT:
1. To issue a memorandum or office order to BTs as officially designate the selected deputized barangay treasurers as deputized collectors;
2. To strengthen the internal control policy over the collection and remittance of community tax fees of barangay treasurers to avoid the incurrence of late remittances and possible misuse or risk of loss of government funds; and
3. To strictly adhere to the guidelines stipulated under Section 29, Chapter 3 of MNGAS, Section 49.H and 49.I of the Local Treasury Operations Manual Vol. II.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the:
1. HRM Officer to:
a. Formulate policies and procedures in the recruitment and engagement of JO personnel to strengthen the internal control pertaining thereto; and
b. Conduct personnel review, assessment and evaluation of the existing manpower complement of the Municipality to determine the necessity and propriety of hiring JO personnel.
2. Municipal Accountant to:
a. Ensure that payments for wages of JO personnel are supported with adequate documentation including duly approved accomplishment reports.

We recommended that the LCE require the LDRRMO to resubmit a complete and updated LDRRMFIP for CY 2024, incorporating both the current year’s appropriations and any unexpended balances from prior years.
We further recommended that the LDRRMO coordinate with the Budget and Accounting Offices to ensure that the LDRRMFIP is consistent with the current year’s approved budget and also incorporates the reprogrammed unexpended balances from prior years, in strict compliance with Section 5.1.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended that the LCE direct the MAO to prepare and maintain the three RAAOs for LDRRMF in compliance with Section 5.1.4 of COA Circ. 2012-002. Obligations made in the utilization of LDRRMF shall be properly accounted for, utilization report showing the correct ending balances shall be submitted to the AT.
We recommended that the LCE instruct the Municipal Accountant (MA) to disclose the amount and details of the LDRRMF unexpended balance for current and prior years in the Notes to FS.

We recommended that the LCE require the LDRRMO to submit the monthly Report on the Sources and Utilization of DRRMF on or before the 15th day after the end of each month in strict compliance with Section 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-002.
We recommended that the LCE require the GSO-Designate to furnish COA copies of Purchase Orders within five working days from issuance thereof for timely review and evaluation.

We recommended that the LCE require the GSO Designate to assign property numbers and tag property stickers to all PPEs owned by the LGU in conformity with Items 5.6, 5.7 and 6.2.5 of COA Circular No. 2020-006. Printing of this property numbers and tags are already being requested to the supplier and upon delivery of such these shall be pasted to every PPEs owned by this LGU.

We recommended that the LCE direct the MA to: strictly comply with the monthly recording of depreciation in accordance with Paragraphs 66 and 71 of IPSAS 17 to ensure fair and accurate presentation of the LGUs financial position and operating performance throughout the year in conformity with Paragraph 27 of IPSAS 1.
To prepare monthly recording of Depreciation on PPEs for the year (Table of DEPRECIATION on PPEs) for the year and shall reflect to this monthly financial statements being submitted to AT.

We recommended that the LCE:
1. Direct the MT to request copies of bank statements of accounts which are not yet enrolled in LBP We-Access on a monthly basis, same with the DBP We-Access accounts, regardless whether the account had any transaction, and forward the same to the MA for preparation of the monthly BRS; and
2. Require the MA to strictly prepare the monthly BRS for all depository accounts and submit the same to the AT within the prescribed period.
We also recommended strict compliance with Section 3.2 of COA Circular No. 96-011 and Section 74 of P.D. 1445.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the Municipal Accounting Office and other concerned LGU personnel to submit to the Audit Team the copies of the MOA/Trust Agreement of the above fund transfers. Henceforth, to ensure the strict submission of the same for the subsequent fund transfers received by the LGU.

We recommended that Management:
1. Require the priority Development Council to propose feasible priority projects that can be implemented within the year, ensuring timely delivery of benefits to the target beneficiaries; 
2. Require the Municipal Planning and Development Officer (MPDO), the Municipal Engineer and other concerned LGU officials to closely monitor the implementation of priority projects out of the 20% DF; and
3. Require the Bids and Awards Committee to expedite the procurement process and direct the Municipal Engineer and other project proponents to implement immediately all unimplemented PPAs embodied in the approved Annual/Supplemental Investment Plan in order to attain the optimal socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes.

1.8	We recommended and Management agreed to instruct the:
a.	Inventory Committee to follow the general guidelines and procedures on inventory taking, recognition of assets found at station, disposition of non-existing/missing PPE items without available record of accountability, and reconciliation of records/reports for the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2020-006; and
b. 	Municipal Accountant and Municipal GSO to prepare and update the PPELCs and PCs, respectively, for each PPE account, and to reconcile their records regularly or every quarter, if possible.

2.10	We recommended and Management agreed to instruct the:
a. 	Inventory Committee to conduct physical count of supplies and materials and prepare and submit the RPCI for each inventory account; and
b.	Municipal Accountant and Municipal GSO to prepare/maintain SLCs and SCs, respectively, for each type of inventory item, and conduct reconciliation of records to determine whether the actual assets agree with the recorded accountability.

3.9 We recommended and Management agreed to instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
a. 	Compute and record the CY 2024 and prior years’ depreciation of the above-mentioned PPE accounts by debiting Prior Years’ Adjustment account and crediting the Accumulated Depreciation account to reflect the correct net book value of the PPE and Government Equity; and
b. 	Henceforth, provide and record the annual depreciation on the PPE accounts in accordance with the guidelines set in Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume 1 and IPSAS 17.

4.7 	We recommended and Management agreed to instruct the Municipal Accountant to strictly follow the perpetual inventory method in the recording of regular purchases of supplies and materials to ensure proper accounting and to promote better control and monitoring in the receipt and issuance of inventories.
5.8 	We recommended and Management agreed to strictly attach DV and ObR in future transactions disbursed through bank credit in order to establish proper accountability and justify the disbursements.

6.7 	We recommended and Management agreed to instruct the Municipal Accountant to expedite the tracing of MOAs covering the unutilized fund transfers presented in Annex C, and/or coordinate with the source agencies to determine its proper disposition.  Otherwise, revert the unutilized balances to the source agencies in accordance with existing rules and regulations.

7.6 	We recommended and Management agreed to submit the duly received distribution lists required for the DVs listed in Annex D otherwise, a Notice of Suspension/Disallowance will be issued for these particular disbursements and to attach duly received distribution lists on future identical disbursements.

8.8 	We recommended and Management agreed to submit the required supporting documents for the DVs stated in Annex E otherwise, a Notice of Suspension/Disallowance will be issued for these particular disbursements and to attach complete supporting documents on future identical disbursements.
16.3	We recommended and the management agreed that necessary action on the settlement of the Notices of Suspensions as of December 31, 2024 in the total amount of ₱0.541 million will be done.

We recommended and Management agreed to the following:
The Municipal Accounting Office review and properly capitalize all eligible borrowing costs directly attributable to qualifying assets and make all necessary adjustments in accordance with IPSAS 5, ensuring the asset's cost and related expenses are accurately reflected in the financial statements; and
The Municipal Accounting Office enhance the Notes to Financial Statements by including comprehensive disclosures on borrowing costs, such as the accounting policy for interest capitalization, key loan terms and details of accrued interest liabilities, to ensure full transparency and compliance with IPSAS 5.

We recommended and Management agreed to the following:
The Municipal Accounting Office prepare accounting entries to reclassify the completed projects from CIP to appropriate PPE accounts and recognize the corresponding depreciation expense and obligation; and
The Municipal Accounting Office and Municipal Engineer’s Office implements a monthly reconciliation process where all ongoing and completed projects are reviewed to ensure timely capitalization of assets into the PPE accounts. This involves the submission of completion certificates and asset verification to ensure all completed projects are capture.

We recommended and Management agreed to the following:
The Municipal Accounting Office ensure the proper recording of all purchases and issuances of semi-expendable property under the appropriate semi-expendable inventory accounts, in accordance with COA Circular Nos. 2022-004 and 2024-006;
The Municipal Accounting Office and the General Services Office (GSO) maintain the prescribed inventory records, including the Semi-Expendable Property Ledger Card, Registry of Semi-Expendable Property Issued, and related reports, to enhance property monitoring and control; and
The General Services Office conduct periodic physical inventories of semi-expendable property and submit the Report on the Physical Count of Semi-Expendable Property to the audit team in accordance with reporting deadlines and COA guidelines.

We recommended and Management agreed to the following:
The Municipal Accountant record all future purchases of supplies and materials in the inventory account at the time of acquisition. Inventory shall only be expensed when it is consumed or used in operations, in compliance with Manual on NGAS for LGUs and IPSAS 1;
The Management establish comprehensive inventory management procedures, including the designation of a stockroom or storage facility, and the maintenance of detailed inventory records for each item. These procedures facilitate proper tracking of inventory consumption, distribution, and disposal; and
The GSO implement a regular physical inventory count to ensure the accuracy of recorded inventory balances and reconcile any discrepancies between actual stock and recorded amounts.

We recommended and Management agreed to the following:
The Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant strictly comply with Section 59 of the NGAS Manual and immediately take corrective action to cancel and reclassify the stale checks in accordance with prescribed procedures;
The Municipal Treasurer issue confirmation letters to the concerned payees to determine the current status of the obligations, and furnish a copy of the confirmations to the audit team for reference and verification; and
Upon verification, the Municipal Accountant prepare the appropriate journal entries to reclassify the stale checks, and remove them from the list of outstanding checks in the BRS.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the Municipal Accountant:
Prepare appropriate adjusting entries to correct the balance reported in the CIP account and the corresponding PPE accounts; and
Recognize the depreciation expenses and the corresponding accumulated depreciation of the affected PPE accounts.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the Municipal Accountant:
Review the balances recorded in the books for derecognition of intangible assets;
Recognize the amortization expense and the corresponding accumulated amortization of the Intangible Assets – Computer Software account; and
Prepare the following journal entries to correct the purchase of computer software instead of a license subscription:
Particulars			Debit		Credit
Other Prepayments			 xxx
Computer Software			 xxx
- To correct entry made in recognizing the asset
Prior Period Adjustment		 xxx
Other Maintenance and Operating Expenses	 xxx
Other Prepayments			 xxx
- To recognize the expense portion of the prepayment

We recommended and the Management agreed that:
The GSO – Designate facilitate the gathering and preparation of all the necessary documents pertaining to the unrecorded PPE, establish the accountability for the PPE and submit the same to the Municipal Accountant for recording in the books; and
The Municipal Accountant ensure the timely and accurate recognition of all PPE supported by appropriate documentation in the books of accounts.

We recommended and the Management agreed that:
The Inventory Committee and the GSO – Designate include all types of properties in the physical count of PPE, submit the RPCPPE that includes all properties and coordinate closely with the Municipal Accountant for the reconciliation of the RPCPPE with the accounting records; and
The Municipality undertake the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances of government agencies as prescribed in COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020.

We recommended and the  Management agreed to:
Adhere to the provisions of COA-DBM Joint Circular No. 2024-1 dated January 30, 2024 on the proper disposal of government properties and dropping from the books of accounts;
Take appropriate steps to restore in the books the unserviceable properties that were prematurely derecognized; and
Ensure proper documentation and record-keeping of government properties.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the Municipal Accountant ensure strict compliance with IPSAS No. 23 and the NGAS Manual by timely setting up Real Property Tax Receivables and Deferred Real Property Tax Income at the beginning of each year and by refraining from directly crediting current year collections to income, in order to uphold the reliability of financial records and the proper implementation of prescribed accounting standards.

We recommended and the Management agreed to the following:
The Municipal Accountant implement the perpetual inventory method, use the SSMI as basis for JEV preparation to recognize the issuances of accountable forms expenses, and maintain SLC for accountable forms; and
The General Services Officer – Designate summarize the RIS, prepare and submit the SSMI to the Accountant to serve as basis in accounting for the issuances of accountable forms, and maintain SC for accountable forms.

We recommended and the Management agreed to establish a robust implementation framework with clear project planning, timely finalization of technical specifications and detailed designs, and secured necessary agreements and property control, while reducing PPA reprogramming and renaming, to ensure the timely implementation of PPAs.

We recommended and the Management agreed to:
Facilitate the completion of site approval requirements through closer coordination with the Sangguniang Bayan and other concerned departments; and
Strengthen project monitoring and establish clear implementation timelines to ensure the timely utilization of the LDRRMF in accordance with Republic Act No. 10121.

We recommended and the Management agreed to adopt strategies to prioritize activities, enhance communication and follow-up with all concerned departments and agencies and ensure the submission of cash advance vouchers for CF and all its supporting documents to the COA ATL within seven days after release of checks for timely evaluation and audit.

We recommended that the Management strictly adhere to the effective date of income reclassification provided under RA No. 11964 and DOF DO No. 074.2024 by recovering the excess payments totaling ₱1.826 million and ensure that the correct salary schedule is implemented only upon the prescribed effectivity date to avoid further incurrence of excessive expenditures.

We recommended and the Management agreed to:
Conduct verification of the existence of the concerned NGOs/POs by coordinating with relevant government regulatory agencies;
Require the submission of the FURs and the full liquidation of the fund transfers granted; and
For NGOs/POs that are confirmed to be no longer in existence, file a request for authority to write-off dormant fund transfers by submitting the necessary documents to the Audit Team in accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We recommended and the Management agreed to:
Implement the projects funded by the fund transfers in order to provide the intended benefits to the public; and
Return the unexpended balances of the fund transfers to the respective source agencies if there are no provisions provided in the MOA that such balances should be retained by the Municipality.

We recommended and the Management agreed to:
Adopt policies and internal controls to monitor loan accommodations and other payroll deductions such that the monthly net take home pay of any personnel of the Municipality does not fall below the mandated minimum amount of ₱5,000.00; and
Strictly comply with the provisions of the succeeding GAAs on the amount of mandatory net take home pay.

We recommended and the Management agreed to the following:
The Management coordinate closely with the Sangguniang Bayan to facilitate the prompt review and approval of the revised Municipal Revenue Code in order to strengthen the Municipality’s revenue generating capacity so that more funds will be available for the implementation of the government’s programs, projects and activities in accordance with RA No. 7160; and
The Sangguniang Bayan give due consideration to the review and approval of the proposed revised Municipal Revenue Code, which has not been updated since CY 2012, in order to enhance the Municipality’s revenue-generating potential to be aligned with prevailing economic conditions and support the sustainable delivery of basic services.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the Municipal Engineer:
Timely submit to the Audit Team the complete information on all ongoing government projects, programs and activities (PPA) as well as those that are to be implemented during the year as required under COA Circular No. 2013-004; and
Coordinate with the Municipal Accountant for the preparation and submission of the monthly project status reports of various government PPAs implemented by the Municipality.

compliance with the requirements of the circular.

Assessor:
a. Trace and assess every component of the Road Networks such as road lots,
road pavement, drainage and slope protection structures, and other
miscellaneous structures and submit the report to the Municipal
Accountant and the General Services Officer.
b. Assess the local road at every reporting date, whether there is any
indication that a component of the local road may be impaired.

a. Segregate and recognize the cost of each component of the local roads
with identified cost as per report of the Municipal Engineer and
Municipal Assessor.
b. Prepare and maintain Road Network Subsidiary Ledgers and separate

d. Prepare a lapsing schedule for the computation of the depreciation tor
each component at the end of the year over the prescribed useful life and

e. Provide disclosures on the total network system in the Notes to Financial

fund transfers, if warranted, in accordance with COA Circular No. 2023-008
dated August 17, 2023. Also, direct:

existence of all dormant accounts;

such as books of accounts, records, financial statements, ledgers,
schedules, vouchers/documents and all other documents;
c. Review, analyze and determine the existence and validity of the

balances.

f. Make a necessary adjusting entries for the correction of the erroneous

a. Provide the Municipal Accountant with a Memorandum of Agreement
(MOA) or established guidelines for the collection of training fees and
counterpart funds for election expenses from barangays to ensure proper
accounting treatment.
b. Collaborate with the Municipal Accountant to trace and account for the
disbursements made for seminars and training programs funded by
barangay collections, ensuring that all expenditures are properly
documented and accounted for. If no sufficient guidelines that would
warrant the recording of the collection as income, any excess should be
returned to the barangay.
c. If no MOA or sufficient guidelines exist for the collection of barangay
counterpart funds for election expenses, initiate the return of the

3.8 We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer make representation with the
bank to determine the nature and purpose of unidentified debit and credit
memos reflected in the Bank Reconciliation Statements of the Municipal
Accountant, and immediately notify the Municipal Accountant of results
thereof.
3.9 We likewise recommended that the Municipal Accountant henceforth review
events that occur between the reporting date and the date when the financial
statements are authorized for issue, and make the necessary adjustments or
disclosures in the financial statements.

4.9 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant reclassify the Accounts
Payable amounting P104,470.00 to the appropriate payable account.
Henceforth, use the payable accounts according to its definition per Annex B of
COA Circular No. 2015-009 in the classification and recognition of payables in
the books of accounts.

5.14 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor order the:
a. Inventory Committee:
i. to prepare an Inventory Reports of PPEs derecognized from the
books, and separate reports for non-existing PPE items and for
unserviceable PPE items.
ii. to conduct of an investigation and determine the last known
location/office where the missing PPE items were issued/installed,
person accountable, circumstances of the loss, and the persons
responsible for the loss, among others.
iii. If accountability/responsibility over the missing PPE is determined
after investigation, demand shall be made from the accountable
personnel to produce the item or to pay the depreciated replacement
cost thereof. If the missing PPE cannot be determined, make a
justification indicating therein the procedures conducted.
b. General Services Officer:
i. Prepare for the ICS for all issued serviceable properties and
equipment.
ii. Prepare an Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Semi-
Expendable Property (IIRUSP) for the inventory of those
unserviceable properties and cause for its proper disposal.

6.15 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor immediately initiate the disposal
of the unserviceable PPEs, and order the:
a. General Services Officer:
i. Prepare a list of non-existing/missing PPEs and identify those with
pending Request for Relief of Accountability.
ii. Issue demand letters signed by the Municipal Mayor addressed to each
accountable officer/personnel concerned demanding the production of
the PPE he/she is accountable for.\niii. After gathering all the necessary information by performing the
preceding procedures, the Property Unit shall submit to the Accounting
Unit the List of Non-Existing/Missing PPEs, with complete information
on which PPEs could not be produced upon demand and those which
have pending Requests for Relief, as well as the corresponding
accountable officers/personnel.
iv. Submit to the Disposal Committee the following accomplished forms,
as appropriate:
 Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property
(IIRUP) listing the properties subject for disposal;
 Waste Materials Report (WMR) - covers expendable materials,
supplies, and consumables, including spare parts, empty containers,
and remnants from destroyed or damaged fixed assets.
b. Disposal Committee:
i. Inspect, appraise, and undertake valuation activities of properties as a
group or individually;
ii. If the property is to be disposed of as junk, verify parts that could be
salvaged or profitably used, e.g., engine, dynamo, wheels, tires, axles,
and body or chassis in the case of motor vehicles;
iii. Set the final appraised value of the properties for disposal based on the
Revised Manual on the Disposal of Government Properties;
iv. Determine the appropriate mode of disposal and recommend the same
to the applicable authorities as enumerated above for approval; and
v. Undertake the disposal proceedings for the properties.
c. Municipal Accountant:
i. Take up the necessary accounting entries to recognize in the books of
accounts the loss of PPE and to set up the corresponding receivables
from concerned accountable officers/personnel for the non-
existing/missing PPEs that could not be produced upon demand; and
ii. Take up the necessary accounting entries for the derecognition of the
PPEs upon disposal based on the IIRUP or WMR, and for taking up
the proceeds from the sale of properties.

7.17 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to direct the Municipal
Accountant:
a. Reconcile the beginning balance recorded in the Subsidiary Ledger of Due
to BIR account for withholding of taxes for government payments with the
actual amount remitted to trace the amount of over-remittance and make

Revenue Regulations and ensure that balances of the month must be
remitted on the following month.
c. Communicate with the Internet Provider for the withholding of CWT and VAT
and require them to issue Invoice for every payment made.
d. Withhold taxes at the prescribed rates for the income payments made to the
speakers and other professional fees regardless of the mode of disbursement/
payment.

Pag-IBIG Contributions and determine the correct amount of contributions to
be remitted in CY 2024, and the immediate remittance of the amount withheld

including the deficiencies.

payroll and the enrollment and update of members in the Pag-IBIG System to
the Office of the Human Resource Management and not with the Office of the
Municipal Treasurer.
8.23 We further recommended that the Office of the Human Resource Management
shall ensure that the records in the Pag-IBIG Fund are updated and the amount
to be withheld in the payroll shall reconcile with the amount to be remitted as
per the system of Pag-IBIG Fund.

of the GSIS Contributions and Loan Amortizations based on the reconciled
data of the Municipal Accountant and the Data Controller in-charge of the
GSIS remittance in CY 2024.

Officer:
a. reconcile records with GSIS and promptly update employees on any

the GSIS Web MPS and prescribed GSIS format to ensure timely and
accurate record updates.
b. monitor the approval of employees' loan applications and immediately
inform the person-in-charge of the payroll and Municipal Accountant to

ensure that all premium contributions and loan amortizations are deducted
from employees' salaries in the payroll for the month they are due.

remittance and the Municipal Accountant:

deductions, and the corresponding remittances as reflected in Tables 21
and 22; and
b. henceforth, conduct monthly reconciliations of employee premium
contributions and loan amortizations by comparing GSIS billings with
actual payroll deductions and remitted amounts. This practice ensures
billing accuracy, facilitates timely remittances to GSIS, and guarantees
correct salary deductions.

coordination with the Municipal Accountant, to thoroughly account for the
beginning balance of the "Due to PhilHealth" account. Any necessary

ensure that all insurable LGU properties are covered under the General

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:
a. direct the MA to draw accounting entries to correct the misstatements in PPE and GE accounts; b. instruct the Inventory Committee to: i. reconstitute with an adequate number of members who can perform the assigned duties and responsibilities, and thereafter devise the PIP for the conduct of physical inventory of all PPE at the most reasonable time, including the preparation of Report on the Physical Count of PPE (RPCPPE) with its pertinent property records, and submit a copy of the same to the AT not later than January 31 of each year. The RPCPPE shall be reconciled with the PPE records or ledger maintained by the MAO and any discrepancy should be adjusted accordingly; and ii. conduct the one-time cleansing of PPE in close coordination with the General Services Office (GSO) and the MAO to establish the veracity and propriety of the balances of PPE accounts; and c. require the GSO to: i. prepare the corresponding Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Property for the identified unserviceable properties that remained recorded in the books, and initiate the process of disposing of the same; ii. maintain efficient property management by following the prescribed procedures in property recording and maintaining accurate property cards; iii. ensure that Property Acknowledgement Receipts are prepared, issued to end-users, and regularly updated/renewed every three years; iv. assign property tags/reference numbers for each PPE item during the physical inventory count to easily identify and trace the location of the subject PPE; v. undergo training on supply and property management being conducted by other government agencies like DBM and COA to keep abreast and broaden his understanding of his responsibilities and accountabilities on property management; d. apply and/or renew the registration of all serviceable motor vehicles with the LTO and contracts of insurance from the GSIS to avoid additional expenses in terms of penalties and payment of damages to third-party liabilities in case of vehicular accident or other casualty; e. monitor regularly if those serviceable motor vehicles are renewed yearly with the LTO and have appropriate insurance coverage from the GSIS; and f. refund the ₱3,060.00 unnecessary expenses representing charges on delinquent registration of serviceable motor vehicles to LTO.

observations.managementAction:

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Management to: a. instruct the OIC-MT to undertake the following courses of action: i. regularly examine the books of accounts of (a) business establishments to determine the truthfulness of the declared gross sales or receipts used in the computation of business taxes paid following Section 171 of R.A. No. 7160; ii. assign personnel at the MTO who are well-equipped with the necessary knowledge and training in the examination of books of accounts to enable the said Office to carry on with their mandate efficiently; and iii. tag all the aforesaid suppliers and contractors who regularly declared more stringent field inspection and be subject to examination of books of accounts to ensure the correctness of taxes, fees, and charges to be collected; b. advise the Business Permit and Licensing Office (BPLO) Chief to implement the following measures: i. continue to require the submission of complete FS and/or documentation of the financial records from taxpayers for assessment of business taxes; ii. prescribe a standard format of Sworn Statement of Gross Sales or Receipts when applicable and require the business taxpayers to have it duly notarized upon submission; iii. coordinate with the ME to identify the contractors who have bunkhouses or other structures that may be considered as project office as a basis for the imposition of business tax; iv. collect business taxes from outside contractors who engaging in various businesses within the Municipality applying the situs of taxation; v. strictly impose the 25% business tax surcharge and 2% monthly interest on delinquent business taxes; vi. submit a written explanation on the noted several cases of inaccurate computations of surcharges and interests for proper disposition by the AT; and vii. require the concerned business taxpayers to pay the business tax deficiency of at least ₱1,886,054.39; and c. require the OIC-MT and the BPLO Chief to devise measures to validate the declared gross sales or receipts of establishments applying for business permits especially those who are having direct transactions with the Municipality and its Barangays.
observations.managementAction: A standard format of Sworn Statement of Gross Sales or Receipts has been provided for the business taxpayers to have it duly notarized upon submission for the business tax validation. Contractors and suppliers who regularly transact with the Municipality were identified and were subject to more stringent field inspection and tax campaign during the year. The OIC-MT and BPLO were instructed to continue the collection/enforcement of business tax deficiencies and to coordinate with ME to identify contractors having bunkhouses or project offices within the Municipality.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management instruct the GAD Focal Point System to: a. undertake a thorough review of the GAD expenditures to identify the specific areas where high expenditures exceeded budgetary limits and to analyze contributing factors behind these occurrences; b. attach the documents required under Paragraph 5 of Section 4.0 (C.8) of JMC 2013-01 to facilitate the monitoring and evaluation of the GAD PPAs implemented during the year; c. observe the deadline for submission of GAD AR to the AT; d. administer the HGDG test whenever it uses attribution in the budgeting phase and AR preparation; and e. seek help from experts and include HGDG training in the budget to ensure the correctness of the GAD issues identified and the responsiveness of the corresponding GAD PPAs.
observations.managementAction: For CY 2024, the Municipality registered a high utilization rate of 89.02% and a commendable implementation rate of 94.44% or 51 out of 54 planned activities. No actions were undertaken during the year to include HGDG training in the budget.
observations.reason: The GAD AR was submitted to the AT only upon request on February 4, 2025; HGDG training inclusion in the budget was not undertaken during the year.
